1: Tola son of Puah, of the tribe of Issachar, arises to save Israel after Abimelech. qûm (H6965, to arise/rise up) — the technical term for YHWH raising up a deliverer (Judges 2:16,18). Tola (tôlāʿ H8439, crimson-worm/scarlet grub) — the name is associated with the scarlet dye produced from the tola worm, used in the tabernacle (Exodus 25:4) and in purification rituals (Leviticus 14:4). The scarlet worm as a deliverance name has been read typologically: Psalms 22:6 — 'I am a worm and not a man' — the psalmist's self-description in the crucifixion psalm. Tola the scarlet-worm judge arises after Abimelech's chaos to restore order, as the Scarlet-wounded Christ rises to restore the broken covenant.
2: Tola judges Israel twenty-three years and is buried in Shamir. His judgeship is the longest consecutive period of peace in the post-Gideon era, yet the narrator gives him only two verses. YHWH's ordinary providential government of history — the faithful leaders who maintain covenant order for decades — receives less narrative attention than the spectacular deliverances and catastrophic failures. The quiet twenty-three years of Tola's judgeship are not less important than Gideon's dramatic defeat of Midian; they are simply less cinematically compelling. The incarnation came in an unremarkable Roman province in a quiet village — not into a spectacular moment.
3: Jair the Gileadite arises and judges Israel twenty-two years. Jair (yāʾîr H2971, possibly 'he enlightens' or 'YHWH enlightens') — a name associated with the Gileadite family of Manasseh (Numbers 32:41; Deuteronomy 3:14). The Gileadite judges (Jair here; Jephthah in Judges 11-12) represent the Transjordanian tribal territory, east of the Jordan, which will face the Ammonite threat directly. Minor judges like Tola and Jair provide continuity between the major judge-narratives — they are the hidden backbone of Israel's covenant governance, unnamed in Hebrews 11 but preserved in God's record.
4: Jair's thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys and held thirty cities called Havvoth-jair. The parallel structure — thirty sons, thirty donkeys, thirty cities — is a sign of prosperity, tribal stability, and effective regional governance. The donkey as a riding animal is significant: it is the mount of peaceful rulers (Zechariah 9:9: 'your king comes to you, humble and mounted on a donkey'), not of war (horses). Jair's sons riding donkeys suggests a peaceable regional administration — an image of the kingdom's shalom that anticipates the messianic king who enters Jerusalem on a donkey to proclaim peace.
5: Jair dies and is buried in Kamon. The minor judges' epitaphs — bare names, brief service durations, burial places — are the OT's version of the 'cloud of witnesses' (Hebrews 12:1): faithful servants whose stories are compressed to essentials because they point beyond themselves to something greater. The burial at Kamon is the final word on Jair: he served, he died, he was buried. This is the trajectory of every judge — they rise, deliver (or maintain), die. The resurrection reverses the trajectory: Christ rose, will never die again, and his death was the death of death (1 Corinthians 15:26).
6: The seventh and most comprehensive apostasy list: Israel did what was evil, serving the Baals and the Ashtaroth, and the gods of Syria, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and the Philistines. They forsook YHWH and did not serve him. The enumeration of seven divine objects — Baals, Ashtaroth, Syria, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, Philistia — is the most comprehensive list of Israel's idolatry in Judges. Seven is the number of completeness: Israel has comprehensively transferred their covenant loyalty to every available alternative. The NT counterpart is Paul's list of sins that exclude from the kingdom (1 Corinthians 6:9-10) and the world's systematic rejection of Christ (John 1:10-11).
7: YHWH's anger burns against Israel and he sells them into the hand of the Philistines and Ammonites. mākar (H4376, to sell/give into the power of) — the same verb as Joseph being sold by his brothers (Genesis 37:28). YHWH 'sells' Israel into bondage as a judicial act: they chose other masters, so YHWH gives them over to those masters. The double oppressor — Philistines and Ammonites on two fronts simultaneously — is the most severe external pressure since the opening of Judges. The compounding of judgment corresponds to the compounding of apostasy: comprehensive idolatry produces comprehensive oppression.
8: The Ammonites crushed and oppressed the people of Israel for eighteen years — the oppression is both intensive ('crushed') and extensive (eighteen years). rāṣaṣ (H7533, to crush/break/oppress) and ʿāṣar (H6231, to oppress/vex) — double verbs for the severity. The eighteen years of Ammonite oppression in Transjordan plus the forty years of Philistine oppression (Judges 13:1) represents the longest sustained foreign domination in Judges. The duration of oppression calibrates to the severity of the apostasy: the more comprehensive the covenant violation, the longer the covenant consequences.
9: The Ammonites cross the Jordan to fight Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim — the oppression spreads from Transjordan into Cisjordan. The expansion of the threat to include the central tribes (Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim) is the escalation that drives the next cry for help. Israel is greatly distressed (wayyēṣer liyiśrāʾēl məʾōḏ — 'and it was very distressing to Israel'). The pattern of distress driving prayer is not accidental: suffering that produces genuine repentance fulfills YHWH's disciplinary purpose. The cross produces the same dynamic: the depth of Christ's suffering reveals the depth of human sin and drives genuine contrition.
10: Israel cries out to YHWH: 'We have sinned against you, because we have forsaken our God and have served the Baals.' The confession is specific and accurate: they name the actual sin (forsaking YHWH, serving Baals) rather than just crying out for help. This is the first clearly articulated confession of sin in Judges — previous cries were more expressions of distress than confessions. The specificity of the confession is important in both Testaments: genuine repentance names the sin, not merely the suffering. 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9).
11: YHWH's response — not immediately comfort but a recital of past deliverances: 'Did I not save you from the Egyptians and from the Amorites... the Ammonites... the Philistines?' The divine cross-examination recalls YHWH's saving history to expose the ingratitude. The Exodus (Egypt), the Amorite wars (Joshua), previous Ammonite and Philistine deliverances — the full span of YHWH's saving acts is listed. This is the pattern of the covenant lawsuit (rîb): YHWH rehearses his faithfulness as the standard against which Israel's unfaithfulness is measured. The NT applies the same logic in the parable of the unmerciful servant (Matthew 18:23-35): the one who has been forgiven much is expected to forgive much.
12: The additional deliverances: Sidonians, Amalek, Maon — oppressors not otherwise mentioned in the Judges narrative, suggesting a wider history of divine rescue than the text explicitly narrates. The breadth of YHWH's past saving acts makes the present apostasy more inexcusable, not less. The accumulated weight of divine faithfulness is also the basis of NT assurance: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' (Romans 8:32). The God who delivered through all those crises will deliver through the final crisis.
13: YHWH's declaration: 'Yet you have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will save you no more.' The divine refusal to save — lōʾ-ʾôsîp ləhôšîaʿ ʾetkem — is the most extreme statement of covenantal withdrawal in Judges. The refusal is conditional and serves a disciplinary purpose: it forces Israel to confront the seriousness of their covenant violation. YHWH does not ultimately abandon Israel (the deliverer will come in Judges 11), but the declaration of withdrawal creates the crisis that drives genuine repentance. Jesus uses the same pattern: the parable of the prodigal's father does not pursue the son — he allows the crisis to deepen until the son 'comes to himself' (Luke 15:17).
14: 'Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen; let them save you in the time of your distress.' The divine irony is devastating: if Israel has chosen other gods, let them experience those gods' actual saving ability. The challenge to the false gods — go to them for help — is the same challenge YHWH issues through Isaiah 41:21-24 (present your case, prove your predictions, do something, good or ill). False gods cannot save; their impotence is proven precisely at the moment when salvation is most needed. The idols that seemed attractive in prosperity prove worthless in distress — which is why Christ's value is demonstrated most clearly in the crucible of suffering.
15: Israel's response to YHWH's refusal: 'We have sinned; do to us whatever seems good to you. Only please deliver us this day.' The surrender — 'do to us whatever seems good' (kaṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkā ʿăśēh-lānû) — is the opposite of the book's recurring 'what is right in his own eyes.' Israel submits their fate to YHWH's judgment rather than their own evaluation. This is the posture of genuine repentance and the opposite of the autonomous self-rule that characterized the entire book. Jesus's Gethsemane prayer echoes this exactly: 'Not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42) — total submission of will to the Father at the moment of greatest distress.
16: They put away the foreign gods and served YHWH, and he became impatient over the misery of Israel (wattiqṣar napšô baʿămal yiśrāʾēl). The phrase qāṣar nepeš — 'his soul was shortened/impatient' — is the same idiom used of Samson (Judges 16:16) where it means reaching one's limit of endurance; applied to YHWH, it means his compassion was so moved by Israel's suffering that he could no longer refrain from acting. This is the interior of the divine character that drives all redemptive action: YHWH cannot bear to watch his people's misery indefinitely. The incarnation is the ultimate expression of this: 'God so loved the world that he gave' (John 3:16) — the divine impatience with human misery that moved heaven to send the Son.
17: The Ammonites encamp in Gilead, and Israel assembles at Mizpah. The geographic setting returns to Transjordan: Gilead and Mizpah are the staging ground for the next judge, Jephthah. The assembly at Mizpah mirrors the assembly at Mizpah in Judges 20-21, but with a different outcome: here Israel gathers for defensive war against an external enemy rather than internal civil conflict. The gathering at Mizpah as a crisis-response assembly will recur in Samuel's era (1 Samuel 7:5-12) — it becomes a symbol of national repentance-and-mobilization. The assembly of the church under persecution mirrors this pattern: crisis drives the community to gather, pray, and seek divine direction.
18: The leaders of Gilead declare: 'Who is the man who will begin to fight against the Ammonites? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead.' The offer of leadership in exchange for military service introduces the Jephthah situation — a negotiated rather than divinely commissioned judge. The conditional kingship offer ('whoever fights, gets to rule') inverts the divine appointment pattern: previous judges were raised by YHWH and then served. Jephthah will negotiate his way into authority — a problematic basis for a deliverer whose tragic flaw (the rash vow) has much to do with his insecure social position. Christ's authority is not negotiated: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18) — given by the Father, not bargained from a crisis.
